title: Case on Impeachment of Chairperson of Korea Communications Commission
text:
1Case on Impeachment of Chairperson of Korea Communications Commission
[2024Hun-Na1, January 23, 2025]
* First Draft
The Court, in a 4-to-4 decision, rejected the claim for removal from office sought in the 
petition for an impeachment trial filed by the National Assembly against Respondent, the 
Chairperson of the Korea Communications Commission. The petition alleged that 
Respondent severely violated the Constitution and statutes during the plenary meeting of the 
Korea Communications Commission on July 31, 2024 by ⅰ) adopting resolutions with only 
two commissioners in office, ⅱ) not recusing herself from an agenda item concerning the 
appointment of executives at the Foundation for Broadcast Culture, ⅲ) participating in the 
resolution on a request to recuse her and dismissing that request, and ⅳ) recommending 
board members for the Korean Broadcasting System and appointing board members for the 
Foundation for Broadcast Culture.
Background of the Case
On July 31, 2024, the President appointed Respondent as the Chairperson of the Korea 
Communications Commission (hereinafter referred to as the “KCC”). 
On the same day, Respondent convened the 34th plenary meeting of the KCC and, with 
three commissioner seats vacant, deliberated and adopted resolutions on the following agenda 
items with two commissioners in office: ⅰ) “Motion for Recusal of a Commissioner,” ⅱ) 
“Recommendation of Board Members for the Korean Broadcasting System and Selection of 
Candidates for Appointment to the Board of the Foundation for Broadcast Culture,” and ⅲ) 
“Recommendation of Board Members for the Korean Broadcasting System and Appointment 
of Executives of the Foundation for Broadcast Culture” (such deliberations and resolutions 
are hereinafter collectively referred to as the “Deliberations and Resolutions at Issue”).
On August 1, 2024, 188 members of the National Assembly proposed an impeachment 
resolution against Respondent on the ground that she violated the Constitution and statutes in 
the course of performing her official duties with respect to the Deliberations and Resolutions 
at Issue. On August 2, 2024, the resolution was passed at the second plenary of the 416th 
session (extraordinary) of the National Assembly, with 186 out of 188 members voting in 
2favor. On August 5, 2024, the impeachment prosecutor filed a petition for an impeachment 
trial against Respondent.
Subject Matter of Review
The subject matter of review in this case is whether KCC Chairperson Lee Jinsook violated 
the Constitution or statutes in the course of performing her official duties and whether a 
decision to remove her from office should be rendered. 
Summary of the Decision
1. Whether Respondent violated the Constitution or statutes in connection with the 
Deliberations and Resolutions at Issue, and, if so, whether such violation justifies 
removal from office
A. Summary of the opinion of rejection by four Justices 
Interpretation of a legal norm must remain strictly within the bounds of what is reasonably 
supported by its text. Thus, it is difficult to regard Article 13, Section (2) of the Act on the 
Establishment and Operation of the Korea Communications Commission (hereinafter referred 
to as the “KCC Act”), which sets the voting requirement of the KCC at a majority of its 
commissioners in office, as allowing an interpretation that, for the adoption of a resolution, 
would require either at least three commissioners in office or a quorum of at least three 
commissioners. Therefore, Respondent’s adoption of resolutions with two commissioners in 
office cannot be regarded as violating Article 13, Section (2) of the KCC Act.
Under Article 14, Section (4) of the KCC Act, Respondent was under no obligation to 
recuse herself. Thus, the impeachment ground based on Respondent’s non-recusal is meritless.
The recusal motions filed by some movants against Respondent were procedurally 
defective for lack of standing, while those filed by the remaining movants constituted an 
abuse of the right to request recusal, as they would have rendered the composition of the 
KCC impossible. Thus, Respondent’s participation in the resolution dismissing the recusal 
motions against herself cannot be regarded as a violation of Article 14, Section (3) of the 
KCC Act.
The legislature has vested the KCC with broad discretion regarding the recommendation 
3and appointment of board members of the Korean Broadcasting System and the Foundation 
for Broadcast Culture. There is insufficient basis to conclude that representativeness or 
expertise was disregarded during the Deliberations and Resolutions at Issue. Therefore, it is 
difficult to find that Respondent’s acts of recommending and appointing those board 
members violated Article 46, Section (3) of the Broadcasting Act or Article 6, Section (4) of 
the Foundation for Broadcast Culture Act.
Accordingly, as Respondent cannot be found to have violated the Constitution or any 
statute in connection with the Deliberations and Resolutions at Issue, the instant claim is 
meritless. 
B. Summary of the opinion of upholding by four Justices 
If Article 13, Section (2) of the KCC Act is interpreted in conformity with the Constitution, 
given both the significance of the freedom of broadcasting guaranteed by Article 21 of the 
Constitution and the legislative intent in establishing the KCC as a collegial body, the 
provision presupposes that the KCC is composed of five commissioners in office and 
provides that resolutions shall be adopted with the approval of a majority of those 
commissioners. Even when, unavoidably, a resolution is adopted with fewer than five 
commissioners in office, it should be regarded as valid only if at least three commissioners 
are in office and a majority of those commissioners approve it. Therefore, Respondent’s 
adoption of the resolutions with only two commissioners in office violates Article 13, Section 
(2) of the KCC Act, which gives concrete effect to the purpose of Article 21 of the 
Constitution. 
Article 13, Section (2) of the KCC Act serves as a key provision ensuring the lawfulness of 
the KCC’s resolutions, and it is among Respondent’s most important official duties to 
conduct deliberations and adopt resolutions in accordance with law. By forcing through the 
Deliberations and Resolutions at Issue while only two commissioners were in office, 
Respondent violated this provision and severely undermined both the authority of the 
Chairperson of the KCC and the citizens’ trust in the public interest and public nature of 
broadcasting. This act, in and of itself, constitutes a grave violation of law warranting 
Respondent’s removal from office, without the need to proceed to a determination on the 
remaining grounds of impeachment.
42. Case in which four Justices rejected and four Justices upheld, leading to a rejection 
decision due to failure to meet the voting threshold required for a removal decision
As to the instant claim, four Justices filed an opinion of rejection, while the remaining four 
filed an opinion of upholding. Because the voting requirement for a decision of removal from 
office in an impeachment trial—namely, the approval of at least six Justices under Article 113, 
Section (1) of the Constitution and Item 1 of the proviso to Article 23, Section (2) of the 
Constitutional Court Act—was not met, the Court issues a decision of rejection.
Summary of Opinion Filed by One Justice Concurring with Opinion of Rejection
As stated in the opinion of rejection, Respondent’s conduct in relation to the Deliberations 
and Resolutions at Issue does not constitute a violation of the Constitution or statutes. Even 
assuming that Respondent’s adoption of resolutions with two commissioners in office 
violated the KCC Act, it is difficult to regard such conduct as constituting grounds sufficient 
to justify Respondent’s removal from office. Therefore, the instant claim is meritless in all 
respects.
Summary of Opinion Filed by Two Justices Concurring with Opinion of Upholding
As stated in the opinion of upholding, Respondent violated Article 13, Section (2) of the 
KCC Act by conducting the Deliberations and Resolutions at Issue with only two 
commissioners in office, which, by itself, constitutes a grave legal violation sufficient to 
justify removal from office. Moreover, since certain movants’ motions for recusal cannot be 
regarded as procedurally defective, Respondent’s participation in the resolution dismissing 
those motions violated Article 14, Section (3) of the KCC Act.
* This translation is provisional and subject to revision.
